cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. The question is about what the history experts' statement proves regarding the "Happy Birthday" song. The options are A through D. First, I need to recall the details from the article. The filmmaker Jennifer Nelson had to pay $1,500 to use the song, which Warner Music Group claims to own. The history part mentions that the original song was written by the Hill sisters in 1893 as "Good Morning to All," and people later changed the lyrics to "Happy Birthday." Experts say there's no record of who wrote the birthday lyrics and that the public was singing it long before it was printed and owned by a company. Nelson's lawyers argue that this history proves the song belongs to everyone, so Warner shouldn't be able to charge fees. Now, the question is about what the history experts' statement proves. Let me look at the options again: A) Does not belong to Warner Music Group B) Has more than 200 years' history C) Was initially owned by another company D) Has always been very popular The experts' statements are that there's no record of who wrote the lyrics and that the public was using it before it was owned by a company. So, the key point here is that the song's history shows it became part of the public domain before Warner claimed it. That would support the idea that Warner doesn't have the copyright, hence the song doesn't belong to them. So option A seems relevant. Option B: The song was written in 1893, which is over 100 years ago, but not 200. So B is incorrect. Option C says it was initially owned by another company. The article says the Hill sisters wrote it, and there's no mention of another company initially owning it. The experts don't say that. So C is probably wrong. Option D: Always
